food_subgroup: Cephalopod seafood dishes — stir-fried whole squid with spicy and sour sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class but no specific entry for Thai-style spicy and sour whole squid preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers squid and cuttlefish as a category. No specific code exists for Thai-style spicy sour squid dish preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888007 refers to squid as a food item generically. No specific code for this preparation style exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid is approximately 350 g, presented as one whole squid with sauce, intended for sharing among 2–3 diners as part of a Zi Char meal.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150); adapted with sauce component estimates from USDA Foundation Foods for chilli-lime sauce ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base squid values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked squid (NDB 15150 / FDC ID 175168). Sauce components (fish sauce, chilli, lime, sugar, oil) estimated using USDA Foundation Foods and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Southeast Asian condiments. Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite of approximately 75% squid flesh and 25% sauce/aromatics by mass, consistent with typical Zi Char whole-squid dish composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — seafood and mixed seafood dishes; squid/shellfish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style spicy sour whole squid. Squid and most seafood dishes with minimal starchy carbohydrate content are consistently classified as low-GI foods. A GI of 30 is assigned by analogy with cooked seafood dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, where protein-dominant seafood preparations with small amounts of sugar and sauce carbohydrates yield GI values in the 25–40 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 19.6) / 100 = 5.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 5.6 × 3.5 = 19.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per sitting, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g means a full restaurant serving (350 g) delivers approximately 1,820 mg sodium — nearly 80% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 30) and a high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional starchy accompaniments; however, the sugar content in the sauce (~2.8 g per 100 g) should be accounted for in total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol content in squid flesh offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 195 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g) and contains beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s; individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume moderate portions occasionally but should be aware of the elevated sodium from fish sauce and the cholesterol load, and should discuss frequency with their clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid is a Southeast Asian seafood dish served at Singaporean and Malaysian Zi Char restaurants, featuring a whole squid cooked in a vibrant Thai-inspired sauce combining fresh chillies, lime juice, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, garlic, and shallots. The dish is typically prepared by wok-frying or stir-frying the cleaned whole squid at high heat to achieve a tender yet slightly chewy texture, then coating it in the aromatic spicy-sour sauce. The flavour profile is bold and complex: intensely sour from lime, fiery from Thai bird's eye chillies, savoury-umami from fish sauce, and subtly sweet from a small amount of palm sugar. The dish is a popular Zi Char staple in Singapore and Malaysia, reflecting the cross-cultural culinary influence of Thai cuisine on the local Chinese seafood restaurant tradition. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-calorie dish with approximately 14.8 g protein and 118 kcal per 100 g. The fat content is relatively low at 4.2 g per 100 g with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (estimated GI 30), making it suitable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. Squid is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine. This dish is typically shared among 2–3 diners as part of a multi-dish Zi Char meal.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked squid data (SR Legacy NDB 15150) with sauce component estimates derived from USDA Foundation Foods and FAO/INFOODS SEA condiment data. The composite weighting (75% squid, 25% sauce) is a reasonable culinary estimate but introduces uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable seafood dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked squid (FDC ID 175168) combined with sauce component estimates; no exact match exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD for this specific dish. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry and is not reliably quantified for this dish preparation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked squid with no hydrogenated fat ingredients in the sauce. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce; the estimate of 520 mg per 100 g reflects typical Zi Char seasoning levels and should be treated as approximate. 6. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Malaysian' and 'Zi Char'; this dish is classified as Malaysian-Thai Fusion Zi Char reflecting its Thai-inspired preparation within a Malaysian/Singaporean restaurant context. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical Zi Char preparation without pork-derived ingredients; individual restaurant practices may vary. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked squid data and are not independently verified for this specific dish preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Applied USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked squid as base nutrient source with sauce component adaptation. Assigned GI 30 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Applied multilingual naming for both Singaporean cuisine rule (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) and Thai-influenced dish rule (Thai script). Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes. Confirmed trans_fat_g = 0.0 is confirmed zero per USDA source. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria satisfied in first iteration.
